flashcards.front: What is the first stage in the graphics pipeline and what does it involve?
flashcards.back: Modeling is the first stage in the graphics pipeline. It involves creating geometric representations of objects using vertices, edges, and polygons. This includes defining the shape, structure, and topology of 3D objects through meshes, curves, or surfaces.

flashcards.front: What operations are performed during the Geometric Processing stage of the graphics pipeline?
flashcards.back: Geometric Processing includes model transformations (scaling, rotation, translation), view transformations (camera positioning), projection transformations (perspective or orthographic), and clipping (removing geometry outside the view frustum). This stage processes vertices and primitives.

flashcards.front: What is rasterization in the graphics pipeline and what does it produce?
flashcards.back: Rasterization is the process of converting geometric primitives (points, lines, triangles) into fragments or pixels. It involves scan conversion and determines which pixels on the screen correspond to each primitive. Each fragment represents a potential pixel with attributes like color, depth, and texture coordinates.

flashcards.front: What operations are performed during Fragment/Display Processing in the graphics pipeline?
flashcards.back: Fragment/Display Processing includes texturing (applying textures to surfaces), blending (combining colors), depth testing (z-buffer operations), scissor tests, stencil tests, and framebuffer operations. This is the final stage before pixels are written to the framebuffer.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between geometric processing and rasterization in the graphics pipeline.
flashcards.back: Geometric Processing works on vertices and primitives (transformations, clipping, projection at vertex level), while Rasterization converts these primitives into fragments/pixels. Geometric processing is vertex-based; rasterization is fragment-based.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the model-view-projection (MVP) matrix in geometric processing?
flashcards.back: The MVP matrix transforms 3D coordinates from model space (object coordinates) to view space (camera coordinates) and then to clip space (projection). It positions objects in the world, applies camera transformation, and projects 3D points onto a 2D screen.

flashcards.front: Why is the z-buffer (depth buffer) important in fragment processing?
flashcards.back: The z-buffer stores depth information for each pixel and is used in depth testing during fragment processing. It ensures that objects closer to the camera obscure objects farther away, providing correct visibility determination and 3D depth perception.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between fragments and pixels in the graphics pipeline?
flashcards.back: Fragments are intermediate data structures generated during rasterization that contain all information needed to update a pixel (color, depth, texture coordinates, etc.). They become pixels only after fragment processing completes and they are written to the framebuffer.
